original_abstract:
Rigid 1,3-disubstituted bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs) serve as invaluable linear bioisosteres for para-substituted benzene rings, offering a strategy to enhance the pharmacokinetic profiles of drug candidates. However, the widespread adoption of BCPs in medicinal chemistry is hindered by the synthetic necessity of using [1.1.1]propellane—a highly unstable and cumbersome reagent that typically requires in-house preparation and immediate use. While more stable alternatives exist, they generally lack the versatile reactivity required for broad application.

In this work, we introduce a class of stable, thianthrenium-based BCP reagents that facilitate practical O-, N-, and C-alkylation reactions. These reagents significantly expand the scope of bicyclopentylation, surpassing the utility of existing methods and even [1.1.1]propellane itself. Their design leverages the unique redox and stereoelectronic properties of the thianthrene scaffold, which enables both the efficient synthesis of the reagents via strain release and their subsequent high reactivity. Upon single-electron reduction, the weak exocyclic C–S bond undergoes selective mesolytic cleavage to generate BCP radicals. These intermediates participate in transition metal-mediated bond formations, allowing for the installation of the BCP motif onto a wide array of functional groups, including the late-stage modification of complex, multistep synthetic intermediates.
humanized_abstract:
The BCP class described as rigid 1,3-disubstituted bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs) can be considered as good linear bioisoster for para-substituted benzene ring with notable biological properties. Although the synthetic application of BCPs is being increasingly explored, their preparation consistently employs [1.1.1]propellane, an unstable and inconvenient molecule, which should always be synthesized in the laboratory before being used. A number of more stable and efficient sources are however now known.

A stable family of thianthrenium-based bicyclic polyaromatic compounds (BCPs) that afford effective and general O-, N-, and C-alkylation are developed. The BCPs herein overcome the limitations of previous bicyclopentylation methodologies and in some cases even surpass the reactivity of [1.1.1]propellane itself, while affording products of unprecedented functional complexity and diversity. The thianthrene backbone serves as a versatile platform for the production of precursors under conditions that are both thermodynamically and kinetically driven by strain release, as well as being a highly reactive reagent upon single electron reduction that activates BCPs to undergo facile mesolytic cleavage of the weak exocyclic C–S bond to yield highly reactive and catalytic quantities of BCP radicals. These species readily undergo a range of transition metal catalysed bond-forming reactions to attach the BCP motif to almost any type of functional group, including sophisticated intermediates of highly multistep synthesis.
